policy_statement: SZ-1 objective: Implement climate-conscious city management, with cooperative leadership by the Municipality taking the lead in climate protection.
mechanism_description: The municipality takes the lead in climate protection through cooperative, climate-conscious city management to support an overall emissions reduction pathway.
primary_causal_pathway: Municipal governance/management → coordinated low-carbon policies & operations → ↓ fossil energy use/↓ high-GHG activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The city alters how it governs—embedding climate objectives into leadership, planning, budgeting, procurement, operations, and cross-department/cross-stakeholder coordination (“cooperative, climate-conscious city management”).  
(2) Mediating process: Institutional and managerial changes increase implementation capacity, align incentives, and accelerate adoption/set of low-carbon measures across sectors (buildings, transport, waste, energy, procurement), leading to reduced fossil fuel combustion and other emission sources.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in community-wide and/or municipal-operations GHG emissions, contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling and driving reductions in citywide GHG emissions through coordinated climate-conscious municipal management.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance reform and coordinated city management are used to implement an emissions reduction pathway, leading to ↓ GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although “better management” can yield efficiency gains (Resource Efficiency) or improved preparedness (Adaptation), the stated purpose and largest climate-relevant effect here is emissions reduction at the city scale.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “climate-conscious management” program were framed primarily around heat/flood risk governance, it would tilt toward Adaptation; if framed around material/energy productivity without explicit emissions goals, it could tilt toward Resource Efficiency. The canonical mechanism explicitly targets an emissions reduction pathway, so Mitigation dominates.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism—municipal leadership/management explicitly oriented to drive an overall emissions reduction pathway—has mitigation as its primary category because its central intended outcome is lowered GHG emissions via coordinated implementation.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific sectors (buildings, transport, waste), operational vs community emissions scope, and the instruments used are policy-specific and require policy-level evidence for any secondary labeling.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Often a meta-policy/enabling mechanism: climate-conscious management can be a wrapper for mixed mitigation/adaptation actions. Classification as Mitigation holds only when the dominant stated objective is an emissions reduction pathway; if the same governance mechanism is explicitly oriented to risk reduction/resilience, it should be re-canonized or reclassified accordingly.
key_indicators: “objective”; “climate conscious city management”; “cooperative city management”; “taking the lead in climate protection”
instance_edge_case_notes: High-level objective without specifying concrete tools (e.g., targets, mandates, funding, procurement rules); classified as a planning/strategy instrument because it describes governance approach rather than an implementable instrument on its own.
